title: VIP / High-Profile Patient — Elevated Access Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.502(a) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.502(a) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.39: VIP / High-Profile Patient — Elevated Access Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.39' running on index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit, lookup vip_patients.csv (PAT_ID, vip_level, start_date, end_date), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" AccessType="PatientChart" earliest=-30d
| lookup vip_patients.csv PAT_ID OUTPUT vip_level, start_date, end_date
| where isnotnull(vip_level) AND _time>=strptime(start_date,"%Y-%m-%d") AND _time<=strptime(end_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| lookup vip_care_team.csv PAT_ID USER_ID OUTPUT is_authorized
| where isnull(is_authorized)
| table _time, USER_ID, PAT_ID, vip_level, Activity
description: Applies extra scrutiny to any user accessing patients on the VIP/high-profile flag list to deter and detect snooping.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes epic and sourcetypes epic:audit, epic:audit`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) VIP list restricted to small privacy group managing lookup; (2) real-time alert to privacy on-call; (3) never place plaintext celebrity names in Splunk—use surrogate IDs only. Indexes required: `index=epic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:audit``.
visualization: Table (masked IDs only), Single value (unauthorized VIP touches). Key fields: `Activity`, `PAT_ID`, `USER_ID`, `vip_level`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `lookup vip_patients.csv` (`PAT_ID`, `vip_level`, `start_date`, `end_date`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) VIP list restricted to small privacy group managing lookup; (2) real-time alert to privacy on-call; (3) never place plaintext celebrity names in Splunk—use surrogate IDs only.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" AccessType="PatientChart" earliest=-30d
| lookup vip_patients.csv PAT_ID OUTPUT vip_level, start_date, end_date
| where isnotnull(vip_level) AND _time>=strptime(start_date,"%Y-%m-%d") AND _time<=strptime(end_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| lookup vip_care_team.csv PAT_ID USER_ID OUTPUT is_authorized
| where isnull(is_authorized)
| table _time, USER_ID, PAT_ID, vip_level, Activity
```

Understanding this SPL

**VIP / High-Profile Patient — Elevated Access Monitoring (§164.502(a) / policy)** — Applies extra scrutiny to any user accessing patients on the VIP/high-profile flag list to deter and detect snooping.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=epic` `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `lookup vip_patients.csv` (`PAT_ID`, `vip_level`, `start_date`, `end_date`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: epic; **sourcetype**: epic:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=epic, sourcetype="epic:audit", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(vip_level) AND _time>=strptime(start_date,"%Y-%m-%d") AND _time<=strptime(end_date,"%Y-%m-%d")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(is_authorized)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: _time, Activity, PAT_ID, USER_ID, vip_level.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (masked IDs only), Single value (unauthorized VIP touches).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for applies extra scrutiny to any user accessing patients on the vip/high-profile flag list to deter and detect snooping. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
